questions.question_text: When you think about your goals and expectations for your birth experience — what you hoped labour and delivery would be like — how clearly could you articulate those goals at the time, and how well did the care system support you in pursuing them?

probes.probe_text: Did you have a birth plan — and if so, how was it received and used by your care team?
Were there goals or preferences you held that felt difficult to express or advocate for in the birth environment?
How did what you wanted for your birth compare to what you were offered or experienced — where were the biggest gaps?
What influenced how you formed your birth preferences — was it previous experience, stories from others, professional advice, or something else?
Were there environmental or institutional factors in the birth setting — staffing levels, protocols, equipment — that constrained what was possible for you?
What has it been like having more frequent monitoring and specialist appointments?
How has being labelled 'high-risk' affected your feelings about the pregnancy?
Do you feel the level of medical attention has been reassuring or anxiety-inducing?
What has it been like having more frequent monitoring and specialist appointments?
How has your medical risk status changed how you relate to your care team?
Have cost or insurance concerns ever affected what care you could access?

questions.question_text: When you think about the decisions you were asked to make during labour and delivery — around pain management, interventions, and procedures — how well-equipped did you feel to participate in those decisions in the moment?

probes.probe_text: Can you describe a decision that was presented to you during labour — how was it explained, how much time did you have, and how did you feel making it?
Were there moments when you felt decisions were made for you rather than with you — and how did that affect you?
How did the decisions made during your birth compare to what you had planned or hoped beforehand?
What role did your partner or support person play in advocating for your preferences during labour?
Were there systemic pressures — understaffing, time constraints, institutional protocols — that you sensed were shaping the decisions being recommended to you?
What has it been like having more frequent monitoring and specialist appointments?
How has being labelled 'high-risk' affected your feelings about the pregnancy?
Do you feel the level of medical attention has been reassuring or anxiety-inducing?

questions.question_text: When you think about the emotional experience of your labour and delivery — from the very beginning through to the moment your baby was born — what were the most powerful feelings, and were you able to process them in the birth environment?

probes.probe_text: Can you take me to a specific moment — what was happening, what did you feel, and who was with you?
Were there emotions during birth that surprised you — feelings you had not anticipated or had hoped not to feel?
How did the care team's manner and communication affect your emotional state during labour?
How did the birth environment itself — the room, the lighting, the sounds, the people present — affect how you felt emotionally?
Looking back, were there emotional needs you had during birth that went unrecognised or unaddressed by the care team?
What has it been like having more frequent monitoring and specialist appointments?
How has being labelled 'high-risk' affected your feelings about the pregnancy?
Do you feel the level of medical attention has been reassuring or anxiety-inducing?

questions.question_text: When you think about the behaviour and communication of your midwives, doctors, and care team during your birth, what interactions stood out — positively or negatively — and what made them significant?

probes.probe_text: Can you describe a specific interaction with a care provider during your birth that you still think about — what happened and why does it stay with you?
Were there moments when you felt dismissed, unheard, or disrespected by a member of the care team — what happened and how did it affect you?
How did the care you received during birth compare to the care you had received throughout your pregnancy from the same or different providers?
Did any provider take time to genuinely explain what was happening to you during labour — and how did that affect your trust and confidence?
Were there patterns in how you were treated that you think might be related to your identity — your background, ethnicity, age, or circumstances?

questions.question_text: When you think about the physical environment of the place where you gave birth — the facilities, the equipment, the space — how did that environment affect your comfort, safety, and overall experience of birth?

probes.probe_text: What specific aspects of the physical environment helped or hindered you during labour and delivery?
How did the birth environment compare to what you had envisioned or hoped for — and how did any difference affect you emotionally?
Did you feel the environment adequately supported the presence and involvement of your partner or support person?
Were there aspects of the birth environment that felt undignified, unsafe, or inadequate — and did you feel able to raise those concerns?
Did resource constraints — understaffing, equipment shortages, overcrowding — feel visible to you as a patient, and how did that affect your confidence in the system?
What has it been like having more frequent monitoring and specialist appointments?
How has being labelled 'high-risk' affected your feelings about the pregnancy?
Do you feel the level of medical attention has been reassuring or anxiety-inducing?

questions.question_text: When you think about any complications, unexpected events, or emergency interventions during your birth, how were these communicated to you, and how did you experience the shift from a planned to an unplanned situation?

probes.probe_text: Can you describe how you were informed about the complication or change in plan — what was said, by whom, and how?
How did you feel in that moment — and what information or support would have helped you feel less frightened or more in control?
Were you given the opportunity to ask questions or was the pace of events such that you could not engage meaningfully in what was happening?
How did the complication or intervention affect your sense of your birth experience overall — and how have you made sense of it since?
Did the system provide any follow-up or debrief after the complication — and if so, was it adequate to your needs?
What has it been like having more frequent monitoring and specialist appointments?
How has being labelled 'high-risk' affected your feelings about the pregnancy?
Do you feel the level of medical attention has been reassuring or anxiety-inducing?

questions.question_text: When you think about your goals for recovery and support in the days and weeks after birth, what did you most want or need — and how well did the postnatal care you received address those needs?

probes.probe_text: What did 'good' postnatal care look like in your mind before you gave birth — and how did that compare to what you actually experienced?
Were there needs you had in the early postpartum period that you felt unable or reluctant to articulate to your care providers?
How consistent was the support you received across different providers — midwives, health visitors, GPs — or did you experience fragmented care?
Were the postnatal checks and appointments focused on you as a person, or did they feel primarily focused on the baby?
Were there systemic gaps — limited home visits, short hospital stays, understaffed services — that left you without the support you needed?
What has it been like having more frequent monitoring and specialist appointments?
How has being labelled 'high-risk' affected your feelings about the pregnancy?
Do you feel the level of medical attention has been reassuring or anxiety-inducing?
What has it been like making pregnancy decisions without a partner?
Who do you plan to have with you during labour, and how did you decide?
Have financial concerns affected your pregnancy choices?
How has your medical risk status changed how you relate to your care team?
Have cost or insurance concerns ever affected what care you could access?

questions.question_text: When you think about feeding your baby — whether through breastfeeding, formula, or a combination — what motivated your choices, and how supported did you feel in pursuing the approach you wanted?

probes.probe_text: What drove your feeding choices — were they primarily personal, medical, cultural, or shaped by pressure from others?
Did you feel judged or pressured — by healthcare providers, family, or social media — in relation to your feeding choices, and how did that affect you?
Was the feeding support you received practical and effective — what worked well and what was missing?
How did your feeding experience compare to what you had planned or hoped for — and how did any gap affect your emotional wellbeing?
Were there structural factors — return to work timelines, lack of private facilities, cost of formula — that affected your feeding decisions?
What has it been like making pregnancy decisions without a partner?
Who do you plan to have with you during labour, and how did you decide?
Have financial concerns affected your pregnancy choices?

questions.question_text: When you think about your emotional and mental health in the weeks and months after birth — the adjustment, the challenges, and the moments of wellbeing — what stands out most, and how well-supported did you feel?

probes.probe_text: Can you describe a period when you felt particularly low, overwhelmed, or unlike yourself — what was happening and who or what helped?
Were there feelings or struggles you were reluctant to disclose to a healthcare provider — and what stopped you from speaking up?
How well did your postnatal care proactively screen for or address your emotional and mental health — was it enough?
How did your experience of your own mental health compare to what you had been prepared for — by professionals, family, or your own research?
Were there wider factors — financial pressures, relationship strain, housing, return to work — that affected your mental health in ways that formal care did not address?
What has it been like having more frequent monitoring and specialist appointments?
How has being labelled 'high-risk' affected your feelings about the pregnancy?
Do you feel the level of medical attention has been reassuring or anxiety-inducing?
What has it been like making pregnancy decisions without a partner?
Who do you plan to have with you during labour, and how did you decide?
Have financial concerns affected your pregnancy choices?

questions.question_text: When you think about the shift in your identity and sense of self after becoming a parent, what tensions or changes were most significant — and how did those changes interact with your other roles, such as partner, professional, or daughter?

probes.probe_text: Can you describe a moment when you felt the tension between your new parental identity and another part of your identity most acutely?
How did the expectations of others — your partner, your family, your employer — shape how you experienced your new identity?
Did your return to work — or the anticipation of it — create particular tensions with your sense of yourself as a new parent?
How did you feel about your body in the postpartum period — and how did that relate to your sense of identity and confidence?
Were there cultural or generational expectations about what kind of mother or parent you should be that shaped how you felt about yourself?
What has it been like making pregnancy decisions without a partner?
Who do you plan to have with you during labour, and how did you decide?
Have financial concerns affected your pregnancy choices?

questions.question_text: When you think about the support you received from your partner in the postpartum period — in caring for the baby, supporting your recovery, and sharing emotional labour — how would you describe that experience, and what would have made it better?

probes.probe_text: How did the division of caregiving responsibilities compare to what you and your partner had discussed or assumed before the birth?
Were there tensions or conflicts with your partner in the postpartum period that were difficult to navigate — what were they about?
How well did the healthcare system include and support your partner in the postnatal care process?
Did your partner's own emotional needs and adjustment get acknowledged by the care system, or was the focus entirely on you and the baby?
Were there structural factors — paternity leave, workplace policies, financial pressures — that shaped how involved your partner was able to be?
What has it been like making pregnancy decisions without a partner?
Who do you plan to have with you during labour, and how did you decide?
Have financial concerns affected your pregnancy choices?

questions.question_text: When you think about the barriers you encountered in accessing postnatal support — whether physical, emotional, informational, or systemic — what were the most significant, and what impact did they have on your recovery and wellbeing?

probes.probe_text: Can you describe a specific instance where you needed help but found it difficult to access — what happened and what got in the way?
Were there barriers that were invisible to your healthcare providers — things they did not ask about or seemed unaware of?
How did you feel when you encountered those barriers — did they lead to frustration, withdrawal, or finding alternative solutions?
Did the formal care system ever feel like a barrier in itself — through its processes, communication styles, or assumptions?
Were any of the barriers you faced specific to your personal circumstances — language, geography, socioeconomic situation, or family structure?
What has it been like having more frequent monitoring and specialist appointments?
How has being labelled 'high-risk' affected your feelings about the pregnancy?
Do you feel the level of medical attention has been reassuring or anxiety-inducing?
What has it been like making pregnancy decisions without a partner?
Who do you plan to have with you during labour, and how did you decide?
Have financial concerns affected your pregnancy choices?

questions.question_text: When you think about how you used digital tools, apps, social media, or online communities after birth — to seek information, find support, or track your recovery — what role did those resources play, and what were their limits?

probes.probe_text: Which specific digital tools or communities did you find most valuable, and what made them useful in ways that formal care was not?
Were there times when information found online caused you anxiety or led you to question your care — how did you navigate that?
How did your use of digital resources in the postpartum period compare to how you used them during pregnancy — did your needs change?
Were there things you searched for or questions you asked in online spaces that you would not or could not ask a healthcare provider — why?
Did digital access — or lack of it — create inequality in the support available to you compared to others in your network?

questions.question_text: When you reflect on your entire maternity journey — from preconception through to the postpartum period — what do you wish had been done differently by the care system, and what single change would have made the greatest difference to your experience?

probes.probe_text: Can you describe the specific moment or phase where you felt most let down by the system — what was the gap and what would have helped?
Was there a particular person or interaction across your whole journey that you feel epitomised what good care should look like — what made them different?
If you imagine a friend going through the same journey, what advice or warnings would you give them to help them navigate the system?
Looking back, were there needs you had that you did not even recognise at the time — things you only understand in retrospect?
What systemic changes — not just individual interactions — do you believe would most improve the maternity care experience for people in circumstances similar to yours?
What has it been like having more frequent monitoring and specialist appointments?
How has being labelled 'high-risk' affected your feelings about the pregnancy?
Do you feel the level of medical attention has been reassuring or anxiety-inducing?
What has it been like making pregnancy decisions without a partner?
Who do you plan to have with you during labour, and how did you decide?
Have financial concerns affected your pregnancy choices?
